worth_it_explanation: Frequency caps that only live at the line-item level do not add up to a meaningful cap for the user. A buyer can stack multiple line items targeting the same audience and each cap is satisfied independently, so the user sees far more ads than any single line item's limit suggests. The fix is structural: a campaign-level or insertion-order-level cap enforces a ceiling across all line items underneath it. These checks take minutes to read inside the Display and Video 360 (DV360) campaign hierarchy. The consequences of not checking can show up in impression waste, audience fatigue, and lower post-click engagement.
intro: Frequency management in DV360 controls how many times a single user sees ads from a campaign over a defined period. A cap set at the line-item level only limits impressions from that one line item. When the same user is targeted by several line items within a campaign, each line item's cap operates independently, and the user can accumulate exposures well past what the advertiser intends. Caps set at the campaign or insertion order (IO) level roll up all line items underneath them and enforce a single ceiling on user exposure. These checks confirm that caps are set at the right level of the hierarchy, that the limits are calibrated to the campaign goal rather than left uncapped or set at a value that provides no practical limit, and that frequency is managed across the different environments where the same user can be reached.

audit_checks.title: Frequency caps are set only at the line-item level, leaving total exposure across line items uncapped
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When caps exist only at the line-item level, a user targeted by multiple line items in the same campaign accumulates exposures from each independently. The intended ceiling is never reached in practice, impressions are wasted on saturated users, and budget that should reach new users is spent on repetitive impressions to the same ones.

educate.base:
DV360 supports frequency caps at three levels of the campaign hierarchy: campaign, insertion order (IO), and line item. A cap at the campaign level applies across every IO and line item beneath it. A cap at the IO level applies across all line items in that IO. A cap set only at the line-item level applies only to impressions from that one line item.

When a campaign contains several line items targeting the same audience, and each line item carries its own cap but no cap exists at the IO or campaign level, a single user can receive one full cap's worth of impressions from each line item. If five line items each allow ten impressions per week, the same user can see 50 impressions per week with no single cap ever appearing to be breached.

A few signs point to this gap being present:
  • Frequency settings on IOs or the campaign are blank or set to 'No limit'
  • Line items targeting the same audience lists carry their own caps but no parent-level cap coordinates them
  • Impression data shows some users accumulating exposures well above the intended per-user limit
You may want to set a cap at the IO or campaign level to establish a ceiling that holds regardless of how many line items run simultaneously. The line-item caps can still exist as a secondary limit per line item; the parent-level cap is what prevents the totals from exceeding the intent.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser running both prospecting and retargeting line items against overlapping audience segments, the absence of an IO-level cap means a user who qualifies for both pools receives the full per-line-item cap from each. Retargeting campaigns especially benefit from a tight IO-level cap since the audiences are smaller and repeat exposure to the same users is more visible.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead-generation campaign may run several line items against a single audience, testing different creatives or bid strategies. Without an IO-level cap, users see ads from each line item at its own limit, which can degrade the impression quality and lift cost per lead as the audience becomes fatigued.

learn_more.source_title: Set frequency caps in Display & Video 360

investigate.intro: Open each active insertion order and confirm whether a frequency cap is set at the IO or campaign level, not only at the line-item level.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Navigate to the advertiser and open the Campaigns list. Select the campaign you are auditing.
Open each active IO within the campaign. In the IO settings, find the Frequency cap section. Note whether a cap is set here or whether it reads 'No limit'.
For any IO where the campaign-level cap or IO-level cap is absent, open the line items within that IO. Confirm each line item's cap and note how many line items target the same audience segments.
Record, for each IO: whether an IO-level or campaign-level cap is present, the limit and time period if one is set, and the count of line items targeting overlapping audiences within that IO.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per IO, whether a frequency cap is set at the IO or campaign level. An IO with a cap at the IO or campaign level that coordinates its line items passes. An IO with no parent-level cap and more than one line item targeting the same audience is a fail recorded with the IO name and the count of overlapping line items.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the frequency cap setting for each IO above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that frequency caps on {{site_name}}'s DV360 campaigns are set only at the line-item level, with no cap at the insertion-order or campaign level to coordinate exposure across line items. Users targeted by multiple line items within the same IO can accumulate the full per-line-item cap from each, so the intended exposure ceiling is never enforced in practice. {{voice_recommend}} adding a frequency cap at the IO or campaign level for each active IO that contains more than one line item targeting the same audience. The existing line-item caps can remain as a secondary limit; the parent-level cap is what closes the gap.

audit_checks.title: Frequency cap is uncapped or set so high it provides no practical limit for the campaign goal
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A cap of 50 impressions per day functions as no cap for most audience sizes. Budget that could reach new users instead rotates repeatedly through the same ones. Post-click engagement rates and conversion rates typically decline as per-user frequency climbs past the point of meaningful exposure.

educate.base:
A frequency cap is only meaningful if its limit is calibrated to the goal of the campaign. A cap that is set to 'No limit', or to a number that is rarely reached given the audience size and budget, imposes no practical constraint on how often a user sees the campaign's ads.

The appropriate limit varies by campaign goal and creative type. Brand-awareness campaigns in display typically run at 3 to 7 impressions per user per week. Retargeting campaigns with a direct-response goal can sustain a slightly higher frequency but still show diminishing returns past 10 to 15 impressions per user per week, depending on the audience size and creative rotation. Connected TV (CTV) inventory carries its own cadence: a viewer seeing the same 30-second ad four or five times in an evening is a distinctly negative experience, so CTV line items warrant tighter caps than display.

A few signs point to a cap being ineffective in practice:
  • The IO or campaign is set to 'No limit'
  • A cap exists but at a level, such as 50 impressions per day, that the auction never reaches for the given budget and audience
  • Reach reports show a small slice of the total audience accounting for a large share of total impressions
You may want to review the cap against the campaign goal and the observed impression-per-user distribution before concluding the setting is intentional.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: Retargeting campaigns for a store often target small, high-intent audiences. Without a calibrated cap, a user who abandoned a cart can see the same ad dozens of times before converting or tuning out. A weekly cap in the range of 7 to 14 impressions is a starting point for most retail retargeting scenarios, adjusted based on observed conversion lag.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead-generation campaigns that target a narrow professional or demographic segment benefit from capping aggressively to preserve audience quality. Running a small audience to exhaustion produces diminishing returns on cost per lead before a replacement audience is ready.

learn_more.source_title: Set frequency caps in Display & Video 360

investigate.intro: For each active IO, confirm what the frequency cap is set to and compare it to the campaign goal and the observed impression distribution.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Open each active IO and note the frequency cap setting: the limit, the time window (lifetime, daily, weekly, monthly), and the level (campaign, IO, or line item).

In the Reach & Frequency report for the IO, check the impression distribution across users if the report is available. A small percentage of users receiving a disproportionate share of impressions suggests the cap is set above what the audience and budget combination naturally produces.

Compare the cap to the campaign goal. For a prospecting display campaign, a limit above 10 impressions per week warrants a note. For a CTV line item, a limit above 5 impressions per week warrants a note. Record the cap setting and your assessment for each IO.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per IO, the cap setting and whether it is calibrated to the campaign goal. An IO with a cap that corresponds to the goal type and is set at a level the campaign can reasonably reach passes. An IO with no cap, or with a cap so high it provides no practical limit given the audience size and budget, is flagged with the IO name, the cap value, and the goal type.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the cap setting and goal type for each IO above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more of {{site_name}}'s DV360 insertion orders carry a frequency cap that provides no practical limit for the campaign goal, either because it is set to 'No limit' or because the stated limit is higher than the audience size and budget combination ever reaches. The result is that a portion of the audience receives far more impressions than the campaign goal warrants, while budget that could reach new users is spent on repeated exposures to the same ones. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the cap on each affected IO against the campaign goal and adjusting to a level that constrains per-user exposure meaningfully, starting with the reach report to see how impressions are distributed across the audience.

audit_checks.title: Frequency is not coordinated across environments, allowing the same user to be reached without a shared limit
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A user reachable on desktop display, mobile web, mobile app, and CTV is counted as a separate user in each environment for most frequency-cap configurations. A campaign with a seven-impressions-per-week cap on display can serve that same seven to the same user across mobile web and again across CTV, tripling the intended exposure with no individual cap appearing breached.

educate.base:
Frequency caps in DV360 are tied to a device or browser identifier by default. A user who moves between a desktop browser, a mobile web browser, a mobile app, and a connected TV (CTV) device appears as a different user to each environment's cookie or device ID. A campaign that caps at seven impressions per week can therefore reach the same person seven times on display, seven times on mobile web, and seven times on CTV, for 21 exposures in a week, without any individual environment's cap being exceeded.

DV360 offers cross-device frequency management through Google's identity graph for signed-in Google users. When the campaign targets Google audiences or uses Customer Match, DV360 can link exposures across environments for users Google can identify and apply a single frequency ceiling across those devices. For the share of the audience Google cannot match across devices, per-device caps still apply independently.

A few signs point to frequency not being coordinated across environments:
  • The IO runs across display, mobile, and CTV line items with no cross-device frequency setting enabled
  • The campaign does not use Google audience segments or Customer Match where cross-device matching would be available
  • Line items are segmented by environment with independent caps but no parent IO-level cap to coordinate them
You may want to confirm whether the campaign's target audience type supports cross-device frequency management and whether the IO-level cap is positioned to act as a ceiling across all environments where the user can be reached.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: Retail advertisers running display, mobile, and CTV together are particularly exposed to this gap. A shopper who saw a product ad on desktop, on a phone, and on a television in the same week experienced three full sequences of impressions that the advertiser may have intended as one. CTV retargeting paired with display retargeting against the same audience segments warrants an IO-level cap and a cross-device frequency check.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead-generation campaigns that run on both desktop and mobile web for the same professional audience should confirm that line items running across environments share a parent IO-level cap. Without it, a prospect receives the full cap from the desktop line item and the full cap from the mobile line item independently.

learn_more.source_title: About cross-device targeting in Display & Video 360
Set frequency caps in Display & Video 360

investigate.intro: Identify IOs that serve across more than one environment and confirm whether a shared frequency cap coordinates exposure across those environments.

dv360_ui.label: DV360 UI
steps: Open each active IO and review the line items underneath it. Note which environments each line item targets: display, mobile web, mobile app, or CTV.

For IOs with line items across multiple environments, confirm whether an IO-level frequency cap is set. An IO-level cap is the primary mechanism for coordinating exposure across line items that run in different environments.

In the IO settings, confirm whether the campaign uses a Google audience segment or Customer Match, which are the audience types eligible for cross-device frequency management. Note whether cross-device frequency is enabled in the IO settings if the option appears.
Record, for each multi-environment IO: the environments represented, whether an IO-level cap is present, and whether the audience type supports cross-device matching.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per IO running across more than one environment, whether an IO-level cap is present to coordinate exposure and whether the audience type supports cross-device frequency management. An IO with a parent-level cap that covers all its line items passes. An IO with line items running across multiple environments and no IO-level cap is flagged with the IO name and the environments involved.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the environment coverage and IO-level cap status for each multi-environment IO above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s DV360 campaigns include insertion orders running across multiple environments, such as display, mobile, and connected TV, without a shared frequency cap coordinating exposure across those environments. Each environment's line item caps independently, so the same user can receive the full cap from each environment with no combined ceiling in place. {{voice_recommend}} adding an IO-level frequency cap to each affected IO so that a single ceiling applies regardless of which environment serves the impression, and confirming whether the audience types in use are eligible for cross-device frequency management in DV360.
